title: Container image digests and deploy reproducibility
summary: Pinning images by digest (@sha256:...) makes deployments byte-reproducible and supports supply-chain audit.
body:
Mutable tags like :latest identify a moving target: the image bytes behind the tag can change without a manifest edit. For production workloads, reference images by digest so that what you approved in CI is exactly what runs in the cluster. Pair digest pinning with your promotion process: build once, sign or attest, scan, then promote the digest through dev/stage/prod GitOps branches.

AegisML's require_image_digest check flags container image fields that lack @sha256:... so teams catch drift before merge.

steps: Build and push the image; copy the registry digest (sha256:...) from the build output or registry UI.
Set image: registry/org/app@sha256:<digest> in the Deployment manifest.
If you must retain a human-readable tag for operators, document it in comments—not as the sole reference.
remediation.verification: kubectl get deploy -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}' and confirm each value contains @sha256:

nist.name: Cryptographic Protection
nist.discussion: Digest pinning supports integrity of software deployed to the platform.

nist.name: Developer Configuration Management
nist.discussion: Immutable references align development artifacts with deployed state.

fedramp.control: CM-5 (Access Restrictions for Change) — operational change tied to approved artifacts
fedramp.notes: Digest-based promotion supports auditable change; map to your SSP narrative.
